content:
# Genesis Chapter 39 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 22v–23r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_039_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_039_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0041 (page 37), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0042 (page 38)

---

## The Man Who Was Taken Down

**1** Joseph had been taken down to Egypt. An Egyptian man named Potiphar — a courtier of Pharaoh and the captain of the guard — bought him from the hands of the Ishmaelites who had brought him there.

---

## The LORD Was With Him

**2** The LORD was with Joseph, and he became a man who succeeded at everything. He lived in the house of his Egyptian master, **3** and when his master saw that the LORD was with him — that the LORD made everything he touched succeed — **4** Joseph found favor in his eyes and became his personal attendant. His master put him in charge of the household, and everything he owned he placed in Joseph's hands. **5** From the moment he made him overseer of his house and all his possessions, the LORD blessed the Egyptian's house for Joseph's sake — the blessing of the LORD spread over everything he owned, in the house and in the field. **6a** So Potiphar left everything in Joseph's care and gave no thought to anything except the food he himself ate.

---

## The Desire That Would Not Be Refused

**6b** Now Joseph was beautiful in form and beautiful in appearance.

**7** Some time after these things, his master's wife fixed her eyes on Joseph and said, "Come to bed with me."

**8** But he refused. He said to his master's wife, "Look — my master doesn't concern himself with anything in the house. Everything he owns he has placed in my hands. **9** In this house, there is no one greater than I, and he has held back nothing from me — except for you, because you are his wife. How could I do something this evil? It would be a sin against God."

**10** And though she spoke to Joseph day after day, he refused to lie beside her or to be with her.

---

## The Garment She Held

**11** One day, Joseph came into the house to do his work, and none of the household servants were there indoors. **12** She grabbed him by his garment and said, "Come to bed with me!" But he left his garment in her hand, pulled away from her, and ran outside.

**13** When she saw that he had left his garment in her hand and fled, **14** she called to the men of her household and said to them, "Look at this — he brought us a Hebrew man to have his way with us! He came to me to sleep with me, and I screamed at the top of my voice. **15** When he heard me screaming for help, he left his garment right there beside me, and he ran and fled outside."

**16** She kept his garment beside her until his master came home, **17** and then she told him the same story: "That Hebrew slave you brought to us came at me to have his way with me. **18** When I screamed out for help, he left his garment right here beside me and ran outside."

---

## Into the Pit of the King

**19** When his master heard the words of his wife — "This is what your slave did to me" — his anger burned hot. **20** Joseph's master took him and threw him into the prison, the place where the king's prisoners were kept. And there he remained, in the prison.

---

## Even There, the LORD Was With Him

**21** But the LORD was with Joseph and extended his covenant-faithfulness to him. He gave him favor in the eyes of the prison warden. **22** The prison warden put Joseph in charge of all the prisoners who were held in the prison — Joseph was responsible for everything that was done there. **23** The prison warden gave no thought to anything under Joseph's authority, because the LORD was with him. Whatever he did, the LORD made it succeed.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### "Was taken down" — the passive voice of Providence

**וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד מִצְרָיְמָה** — "And Joseph was taken down to Egypt."

Chapter 39 opens on a passive verb. Not *Joseph went down to Egypt*, but *Joseph was taken down* — the Hophal of *yarad*, a causative passive: someone caused this descent to happen to him, and he had no say in the matter. The verb choice is precise and theologically loaded. In Hebrew narrative, the active and passive voices are rarely interchangeable — when the narrator chooses the passive, it is because agency matters. Joseph did not choose this descent. He was carried, trafficked, sold. He arrived in Egypt not as an immigrant or an adventurer but as a commodity.

The word *hurad* — "was taken down" — is the same root (*yarad*) that drives the descent motif throughout this section of Genesis. Jacob "went down" into grief when he saw Joseph's robe (37:35 — *vayered*). Judah "went down" from his brothers (38:1 — *vayyered*). Joseph "was brought down" to the Ishmaelites (37:25, 28). Now Joseph "was taken down" to Egypt. The entire movement of these chapters is downward — geographic, social, familial. Everything is descending.

And yet the chapter immediately introduces the counter-force to this descent: **וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת-יוֹסֵף** — "And the LORD was with Joseph." The downward movement of the narrative and the upward movement of divine accompaniment are placed side by side within three verses. The grammar of the chapter is theodicy. Joseph is being taken down; the LORD is going with him.

This is not a theology of divine rescue — at least not yet. The LORD does not reverse the descent in chapter 39. Joseph ends the chapter in prison. But the LORD's presence transforms the experience of the descent: things succeed, favor is extended, even the prison warden begins to trust him. The pattern is not "God prevents suffering" but "God accompanies and redeems suffering from within." This distinction is essential to reading the Joseph narrative correctly, and the chapter makes it clear from verse 2.

---

### The LORD was with Joseph — four times

**וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת-יוֹסֵף** — "The LORD was with Joseph."

This is the theological refrain of the chapter, and the narrator strikes it four times — twice at the beginning (vv. 2, 3) and twice at the end (vv. 21, 23), bracketing the entire drama of temptation, false accusation, and imprisonment within the same announcement. The structure is deliberate:

- *The LORD was with Joseph* (v. 2)
- *His master saw that the LORD was with him* (v. 3)
- [Everything happens — garment, accusation, prison]
- *The LORD was with Joseph and extended his chesed to him* (v. 21)
- *The LORD was with him; whatever he did, the LORD made it succeed* (v. 23)

The refrain functions as a frame. It insists that the reader interpret everything within the chapter — including everything that goes wrong — in light of this fact. The assault, the false accusation, the prison: these events are not evidence of divine abandonment. They are events that occur inside a story whose governing reality is divine presence.

This makes Genesis 39 one of the most sustained meditations on the relationship between suffering and Providence in the entire Bible. The man who experiences the worst the chapter has to offer is specifically identified as the man the LORD is with. The chapter refuses to let those two realities cancel each other out.

The refrain also echoes back to the patriarchal covenant formula. "I will be with you" is the characteristic language of divine promise in Genesis — God says it to Isaac (26:3, 24), to Jacob (28:15; 31:3), to the patriarchs generally. Here it is not spoken as a promise but reported as a fact. Joseph has not been given the covenant in a dream the way his ancestors were. No theophany marks his life. The promise simply occurs — enacted in the texture of daily events, visible to the Egyptian slave-master who has no interest in theology and simply notices that things work when Joseph is in charge.

That Potiphar can see it — that a pagan Egyptian observes *the LORD was with him* — is itself significant. The divine presence is not hidden or mystical or accessible only to the initiated. It is visible in the pattern of outcomes. This is the narrative's most pragmatic account of divine blessing: even the unbeliever can see that this man's work succeeds in an unusual way.

---

### Beautiful in form and beautiful in appearance — the double curse

**וַיְהִי יוֹסֵף יְפֵה-תֹאַר וִיפֵה מַרְאֶה** — "Now Joseph was beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance."

This description is introduced by the narrator in verse 6b, after the account of Joseph's successful management of Potiphar's household, and before the account of Potiphar's wife's proposition in verse 7. Its placement is structural: the beauty is the hinge between success and danger.

The phrase is verbally identical to the description of Joseph's mother Rachel in Genesis 29:17: **יְפַת-תֹּאַר וִיפַת מַרְאֶה** — "beautiful of form and beautiful of appearance." Joseph looks like his mother. In a genealogical narrative this interested in how traits pass from parent to child, the echo is not accidental. Rachel's beauty was a magnet that drew Jacob to her across the ancient Near East and through seven years of labor. Joseph's beauty, inherited from her, will now draw a different kind of unwanted attention — and bring him to ruin in a household that should have been safe.

Beauty in the biblical narrative is regularly a source of narrative danger. It is what makes Sarah vulnerable to Pharaoh (12:11-15) and to Abimelech (20:2). It is what draws Shechem to Dinah (34:2). It is what draws David's eye to Bathsheba (2 Samuel 11:2). Joseph's beauty is the precondition for the assault that follows, and the narrator flags it precisely there, at the threshold between his rise and his crisis, so the reader sees the connection. He was prosperous; he was beautiful; and immediately: his master's wife lifted her eyes toward him and said, "Come to bed with me."

The irony is structural. The same feature that in other patriarchal narratives threatens the woman is here directed at a man. Joseph's vulnerability is gendered opposite to the usual pattern. He is the beautiful object of an aggressive and predatory desire, and his danger comes not from his position of weakness — he has risen to a position of considerable authority — but from precisely that beauty and precisely that authority. The wife of the man he serves wants what she cannot have.

---

### "How could I sin against God?" — the first theology of sexual ethics

**וְחָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים** — "And I would sin against God."

Joseph's refusal in verses 8-9 is one of the most carefully constructed speeches in the Joseph narrative, and its climax is not a statement about loyalty to Potiphar — though that is where the speech begins — but a statement about God.

He begins with the social and legal dimension: Potiphar has entrusted everything to him, held back nothing. He catalogs the trust that has been placed in him: the household, the possessions, the authority. He establishes the relational framework. This is a gift that cannot be betrayed.

But then he pivots. The final statement is not "I would betray my master" or "this is disloyal to the man who trusts me." It is: **וְחָטָאתִי לֵאלֹהִים** — "I would sin against God." The preposition is *le-* — a sin *directed toward* God, *against* God. This is not the language of rule-breaking or social transgression. It is the language of relational offense — the kind of sin that reaches upward, that wrongs not only the human parties involved but the divine one.

This is the first explicit statement in the Joseph narrative that positions God as the one offended by moral transgression. It is also, in some sense, the first statement in Genesis of a theology of sexual ethics that is grounded not in social contract or legal obligation but in the character of God. Joseph does not say: "It would be illegal." He does not say: "Potiphar would find out." He says: "It would be a sin against God."

The statement has a double function. It reveals Joseph's moral formation — wherever he received it (his father's house, his own reflection, his experience of the LORD's presence), Joseph has arrived at a position where the divine is the ultimate moral court of appeal. And it also contrasts directly with what has preceded it in Genesis 38. Judah's moral failures in chapter 38 — the withholding of Shelah, the approach of the roadside woman — are never framed in terms of sin against God. They are addressed by the narrative through consequences, through Tamar's intervention, through the humiliation of recognition. But Judah himself never says: *this would be a sin against God.*

Joseph says it. And he says it before anything has happened. This is not repentance after the fact — it is moral clarity before the act.

---

### The garment — the second false witness

**וַיַּעֲזֹב בִּגְדוֹ בְּיָדָהּ** — "He left his garment in her hand."

The word *beged* — garment — appears seven times in verses 12-16 in various forms. The repetition is obsessive, almost mechanical. The narrator will not let the reader look away from the garment. It is grabbed, held, abandoned, kept, displayed, produced as evidence. The garment is the mechanism of the accusation, the prop of the lie, the instrument of Joseph's destruction.

It is also the second garment in the Joseph narrative used as false evidence against Joseph.

The first was the *ketonet passim* — the long, ornamented robe of chapter 37, the garment Jacob gave Joseph and that his brothers stripped from him, soaked in goat's blood, and brought to their father as false proof of Joseph's death. **הַכֶּר-נָא** — "Please recognize" — they said, and Jacob recognized it and was destroyed by the recognition.

Here, the *beged* — a simpler word for garment, a working garment, the ordinary clothing of a household manager — is seized from Joseph's body and held by a woman who constructs from it an accusation of attempted rape. The garment proves nothing except that Joseph was there and left in a hurry. But in the economy of the accusation, the garment is everything. She displays it to the household servants (v. 14-15) and then to her husband (v. 16-18). It serves as exhibit A in a case that has no other witness.

Two garments. Two false accusations. Two people destroyed by what was offered as evidence. This is the pattern the narrator has woven through chapters 37 and 39, and the echo is unmistakable. Joseph was convicted once by his brothers using cloth and blood. He is convicted again by a woman using cloth and flight. Both times the garment belongs to him. Both times it is taken from him. Both times it is used to tell a story that is the exact opposite of what actually happened.

The reader who has been following closely feels the weight of the echo. The repetition of the garment motif is not accidental literary decoration — it is the narrator's way of insisting that what is happening to Joseph has a pattern, that the persecution is systemic rather than episodic, that Joseph moves through a world in which the instruments of his identity are repeatedly stripped from him and used against him.

---

### "That Hebrew man" — the weaponized stranger

**הָאִישׁ הָעִבְרִי** — "that Hebrew man."

Potiphar's wife uses this phrase twice — once to the household servants (v. 14) and once to her husband (v. 17). The designation *ish ivri* — "a Hebrew man" — is not neutral. In each case it is paired with a verb of violation: *he brought him to mock us*, *the Hebrew slave you brought*, *to have his way with us*.

The term "Hebrew" (*ivri*) in the ancestral narratives marks ethnic otherness from the perspective of Egyptians or Canaanites. It is how outsiders name the patriarchal clan — how Joseph is identified in chapter 40 by the cupbearer who has shared a prison with him (40:15), how the Egyptians identify the group in 43:32. It is not, in these texts, a term of self-identification: Joseph does not call himself a Hebrew. Others call him one, and they do so in contexts where the designation carries the force of: *not one of us, not bound by our social obligations, capable of offenses our own people would not commit.*

Potiphar's wife wields the designation as a weapon. By making Joseph's ethnicity the first fact she announces — "He brought us a *Hebrew man*!" — she places his alleged crime in a framework of ethnic danger. The Hebrew is the threat from outside. The accusation is not merely personal — *this man assaulted me* — but ethnic: *this Hebrew, this outsider, is what happens when you bring foreign men into an Egyptian house*. The slander works at two levels simultaneously.

The narrative irony is devastating. Potiphar's wife, in her accusation, calls Joseph an outsider threatening the household. But it is she who is threatening the household. She is the one violating the boundaries of marital loyalty and trust. She is the one whose behavior would, if exposed, destabilize everything. Joseph, the outsider, is the one who has been protecting the household's integrity — refusing the proposition precisely because of his loyalty to the man who trusted him. The accusation has inverted reality completely, and the ethnic frame makes the inversion easier to believe.

---

### Potiphar's anger — and what he does not do

**וַיִּחַר אַף אֲדֹנָיו** — "And his master's anger burned hot."

The text is economical here in a way that has intrigued readers for centuries. Potiphar hears his wife's story, his anger burns, and he throws Joseph into prison. The narrative does not linger on the confrontation. It does not tell us whether Potiphar believed his wife completely, or whether doubt flickered through his mind. It tells us only about the anger and the action.

But careful readers have noticed something: the punishment does not fit the crime as charged. If an Egyptian slave had genuinely attempted to rape the wife of a high-ranking royal official — the captain of the guard — the expected punishment in Egyptian law would have been death. Not prison. The prison to which Joseph is consigned is, moreover, a royal prison — the place where the king's own prisoners are kept — which implies some degree of official standing in the facility, not the simple disposal of a criminal slave.

Several ancient and modern interpreters have concluded from this that Potiphar did not entirely believe his wife, or that his anger was as much about the disruption and embarrassment as about the crime itself, and that he chose the prison as a response that punished Joseph while avoiding the administrative and social complications of an execution. The text does not say this. But the text's silence is itself a kind of statement — the narrator does not want to resolve the question definitively.

What the text does say is that the LORD was with Joseph *even there* — in the prison, under the false charge, in the place where everything has descended as low as it can go. The injustice is not reversed; it is accompanied.

---

### Chesed in the prison — covenant-faithfulness in an impossible place

**וַיֵּט אֵלָיו חָסֶד** — "And he extended chesed to him."

The word *chesed* — covenant-faithfulness, loving-kindness, loyal love — appears here at verse 21 in a context that is startling. *Chesed* is the word for the particular quality of loyalty that belongs to covenant relationships: the love that keeps faith, that does not abandon, that continues when circumstances provide every reason to withdraw. It is the word Jacob uses when asking for a burial in Canaan (47:29). It is the word the servant uses when praising God for faithfulness to Abraham (24:12, 14, 27, 49). It is the word Ruth will use to describe her loyalty to Naomi (Ruth 1:8; 3:10).

Here, God extends *chesed* to Joseph in a prison. The word that belongs to covenant relationship, to the loyalty of allies and lovers and family members — this word is applied to what God does for Joseph when Joseph has nothing. No freedom, no status, no resources, no legal recourse. Just a prison cell and a false charge.

The theological statement is bold. God's *chesed* does not require favorable conditions. It does not wait for restoration or prosperity to announce itself. It shows up *there*, in the lowest point of the narrative's descent, in the same spirit in which it appeared at the top of the chapter: *the LORD was with Joseph.* The content of divine accompaniment is *chesed* — the covenant-faithful love that is not conditional on outcome.

And the effect of this *chesed* is practical: Joseph finds favor in the eyes of the prison warden. The mechanism is the same as in Potiphar's house: the LORD's presence with Joseph becomes visible in Joseph's effectiveness, and that effectiveness generates trust in the man who oversees him. What works at the level of the estate works at the level of the prison. The pattern is fractal — the same dynamic repeats at every scale, in every context, regardless of what Joseph's social status is at the moment.

This is the chapter's most subversive argument. Success, favor, and divine accompaniment are not dependent on freedom. Joseph in prison is just as much the man the LORD is with as Joseph the estate manager. The prison does not end the story. It is another phase of the same story — the same God, the same presence, the same pattern of blessing that accrues in unexpected directions.

---

### The descent and the hidden ascent — the shape of the Joseph narrative

Genesis 39 is the chapter that establishes what the entire Joseph narrative will do. Every time the story looks like it has hit bottom, it is actually preparing for an ascent. Every instrument of Joseph's destruction becomes, in the long arc of the narrative, a vehicle for his advancement. Sold by his brothers: he becomes Potiphar's overseer. Accused by Potiphar's wife: he enters the prison where he will meet Pharaoh's cupbearer. Forgotten by the cupbearer (40:23): he waits two years until Pharaoh needs a dream interpreter.

The shape of the narrative is not a straight line from suffering to triumph. It is a series of descents each of which conceals an ascent. And the theological argument being made — quietly, persistently, without a single divine speech or theophany in this chapter — is that this is what Providence looks like from inside. Not a clear upward path, but a descent that is somehow always accompanied, always productive, always going somewhere even when no destination is visible.

Joseph in chapter 39 does not know any of this. He does not know that the prison will be the place where he meets the cupbearer. He does not know that his integrity in refusing Potiphar's wife will be remembered by no one for years. He does not know that the LORD's presence, which is invisible to him, is visible to every Egyptian who observes his work. He knows only that he refused what he knew was wrong, was punished for it, and is now in a cell.

The reader knows more. And the gap between what Joseph knows and what the reader knows is precisely where the theology of the chapter lives. The reader, watching the pattern repeat — *the LORD was with Joseph* (vv. 2, 21) — can see the shape of what is happening even while Joseph cannot. This is the narrator's most careful gift to the audience: the ability to trust the arc even in the dark sections, because the pattern has been shown to us and we have been invited to believe it holds even when the character inside it cannot see it.

---

### Joseph and Judah — the contrasting siblings

The placement of Genesis 39 immediately after Genesis 38 is a sustained moral comparison. The two chapters function as a diptych.

In Genesis 38, Judah — the brother who proposed selling Joseph — descends morally and relationally. He withholds justice from Tamar. He approaches a roadside woman casually. He sentences his daughter-in-law to death by burning without a full hearing. His moral instincts, throughout the chapter, run toward self-protection and the exercise of power in his own favor. His transformation, when it comes, comes from outside — Tamar forces the recognition; Judah does not arrive at it himself.

In Genesis 39, Joseph — the brother who was sold — is presented with a clear moral temptation and refuses it categorically. His refusal is grounded in loyalty (to his master), in a sense of the moral order (this would be evil), and finally in theology (it would be a sin against God). He refuses not once but persistently, day after day, throughout verse 10's understated summary of sustained pressure. He is given every opportunity to rationalize — he is an enslaved man with no legal standing, his master is absent, no one would know, he is not responsible for the system that has placed him in this position — and he takes none of them.

The contrast is not subtle. The narrator has placed Judah's moral failure and Joseph's moral integrity side by side and allowed the comparison to speak for itself. The brothers who sold Joseph were not morally superior to the brother they sold. Joseph, who came out of the same family, the same father, the same history of favoritism and jealousy, has arrived somewhere the others have not.

The question the narrative leaves open is why. What has formed Joseph's moral character? The text does not explain. It simply shows the outcome: one brother descends morally while descending geographically; the other maintains his integrity while being driven down involuntarily into slavery and prison. The same God is present in both stories, but the moral responses of the two men could not be more different.

This contrast sets up the climax of the Joseph narrative in chapter 44, where Judah — who has by then undergone his own transformation — will offer himself as a slave in place of Benjamin. The two arcs will eventually meet. But in chapter 39, they are at their point of maximum divergence: Judah at Enaim with a veiled woman, Joseph in Potiphar's house saying no.

---

### Manuscript observations

The Leningrad Codex folio 22v (GEN_039_page_001.jpg) carries the final portion of Genesis 38 in its upper columns and transitions into Genesis 39 in the lower portion of the right column. The chapter transition from 38 to 39 is marked with a parasha division indicator — the characteristic *samekh* or *peh* in the Masoretic tradition — visible in the right margin column. The opening words of Genesis 39 — **וְיוֹסֵף הוּרַד מִצְרָיְמָה** — are legible with full Tiberian vocalization, the Hophal pointing of *hurad* clearly visible, and the characteristic *waw* consecutive on the following verb **וַיִּקְנֵהוּ** ("and he bought him").

The key phrase in verse 2 — **וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת-יוֹסֵף** ("and the LORD was with Joseph") — carries the full Tetragrammaton יְהוָה with Tiberian pointing. The maqqef connecting **אֶת-יוֹסֵף** is clearly visible, linking the preposition to the name. The verbal construction **וַיְהִי אִישׁ מַצְלִיחַ** in verse 2 — "and he was a successful man / a man who prospered" — features the Hiphil participle *matsliach* (of *tsalach*, to succeed, to prosper), also clearly pointed.

The word **בִּגְדוֹ** ("his garment") in verse 12, and its repeated appearances through verse 18, are visible in the middle columns of folio 22v. The repetition of *beged* in its various suffixed forms throughout this passage is striking even on the manuscript page, where the same root consonants appear again and again within a short textual space.

Folio 23r (GEN_039_page_002.jpg) opens at approximately verse 16 and carries the remainder of the chapter through verse 23 and into the opening of Genesis 40. The folio number **23** is visible at the top of the page in the West Semitic Research Project image. The phrase **וַיְהִי יְהוָה אֶת-יוֹסֵף** in verse 21 — the reprise of the opening refrain — is legible in the left column, the Tetragrammaton again in its Tiberian form. The word **חֶסֶד** in verse 21 is pointed with the characteristic *segol-segol* pattern of the noun form, the *chet* marked with its dot, clearly readable.

The concluding phrase of the chapter in verse 23 — **כִּי יְהוָה אִתּוֹ וַאֲשֶׁר-הוּא עֹשֶׂה יְהוָה מַצְלִיחַ** ("because the LORD was with him, and whatever he did, the LORD made succeed") — brings back the same *matsliach* of verse 2 in an inclusio, the same Hiphil participle of *tsalach* closing the chapter that opened it. The verbal echo is visible on the page.

The Codex Vaticanus page 37 (pa_0037; GEN_039_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the chapter heading **CAP XLIII** in the right margin — continuing the Vaticanus chapter numbering that does not correspond to the modern chapter divisions — and the Greek text runs continuously in its uncial hand. The Genesis 39 material occupies the central and right columns of page 37. Page 38 (pa_0038; GEN_039_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the concluding portion of Genesis 39 and transitions into Genesis 40, marked in the right margin by the notation **CAP XLV**.

Notable LXX (Vaticanus) variants: **v.1** — Vaticanus reads **εἰς Αἴγυπτον** ("into Egypt") with the same directional force as the MT, but renders *Potiphar* as **Πετεφρης** throughout, a slightly different Greek transliteration than the standard *Potipha* of LXX tradition; **v.2** — the phrase **ἦν κύριος μετὰ Ιωσηφ** ("the Lord was with Joseph") renders the Tetragrammaton as *kyrios*, as is standard in the LXX, using the Greek preposition *meta* ("with") for the Hebrew preposition *et* in its locative sense — a common LXX strategy; **v.6** — Vaticanus renders the double beauty description **καλὸς τῷ εἴδει καὶ ὡραῖος τῇ ὄψει** ("beautiful in form and blooming in appearance") — the word *hōraios* ("blooming, in the prime of beauty") is slightly different from the more literal rendition and carries a nuance of youthful or seasonal beauty; **v.9** — the phrase **καὶ ἁμαρτήσομαι ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ** ("and I will sin before/in the presence of God") renders the Hebrew *chatati le'Elohim* with the preposition *enantion* — "in the presence of, before" — which gives the sin a more spatial or relational quality than the simple *against*; **v.14** — Vaticanus renders *ish ivri* as **ἄνθρωπον Εβραῖον** — "a Hebrew person/man," using *anthrōpos* (person, human) rather than *anēr* (man in the male-gender-specific sense), a flattening of the gendered specificity of the Hebrew; **v.21** — the phrase **ἔδωκεν αὐτῷ χάριν** ("gave him grace/favor") renders the Hebrew *chesed* not with the expected *eleos* (mercy, covenant-love) but with *charis* (grace, favor) — an interesting choice that emphasizes the gift-quality of divine benevolence in the prison context; **v.23** — the final phrase mirrors the opening refrain, with *kyrios* used for the Tetragrammaton, and the Hiphil participle *matsliach* rendered by a form of the Greek verb *εὐοδόω* (*euodoō*, to prosper, to succeed in one's journey) — an interesting translation that brings a traveler's connotation to the success-language.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 22v–23r, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folio 22v (GEN_039_page_001.jpg, PDF page 43) carries the end of Genesis 38 through approximately Genesis 39:15-16. The parasha section marker is visible between chapters 38 and 39. Folio 23r (GEN_039_page_002.jpg, PDF page 44) carries Genesis 39:16 through verse 23 and opens Genesis 40. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 37–38 (pa_0037–pa_0038); accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0041 and /0042.*
